description: The smooth hammerhead is one of the larger hammerhead sharks, named for its smooth-edged, evenly curved cephalofoil that lacks the central indentation found in other hammerheads. It ranges widely across temperate and tropical seas and is one of the more commonly encountered hammerhead species. Young sharks are often found in large schools in inshore waters.
funFact: Unlike most hammerhead species that prefer warm tropical waters, the smooth hammerhead is one of the most cold-tolerant hammerheads, venturing into waters as cool as 8°C (46°F) — and newborn pups are frequently found in temperate estuaries and bays far from the tropics.
habitat: Coastal and offshore waters in tropical and temperate seas worldwide, often in shallower inshore areas, estuaries, and bays. More tolerant of cooler waters than other hammerhead species; found from the surface to at least 200 m depth.
geographicRangeDescription: Circumglobal in temperate and subtropical coastal waters, ranging farther from the equator than other hammerhead species. Found along both coasts of the Americas, around southern Africa, southern Australia, the Mediterranean, and parts of the western Pacific. Migrates seasonally toward cooler high-latitude waters in summer, including the waters around New England and the British Isles.
diet: An opportunistic apex predator feeding primarily on bony fish, squid, octopus, and smaller sharks and rays. Like other hammerheads, uses its wide cephalofoil to detect the electrical fields of buried stingrays and flatfish hiding in sediment.
size: Typically 2.5–3.5 m (8.2–11.5 ft); max confirmed ~4.0 m (13.1 ft). Females grow larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — significantly depleted in all heavily fished regions

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 20–50 pups per litter; ~10–11 month gestation; breeds every 2 years

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Bony fish, squid, octopus, stingrays, smaller sharks

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Low — late maturity and biennial reproduction limit recovery from fishing pressure

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Smooth-edged cephalofoil packed with electroreceptors for detecting buried prey; cold-tolerance allows exploitation of temperate nursery habitats unavailable to other hammerhead species

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and temperate coastal and offshore waters worldwide — Atlantic, Pacific, Indian Ocean, and Mediterranean; one of the widest-ranging hammerhead species

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~200 m (656 ft); juveniles frequently in shallow inshore and estuarine habitats

threats.name: Shark Fin Trade
threats.description: Smooth hammerhead fins are large and highly valuable in the international fin trade. Hammerhead fins are among the most traded globally, and smooth hammerheads are frequently landed alongside scalloped and great hammerheads with minimal species-level distinction.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Routinely caught in pelagic and coastal longlines, drift gillnets, and trawls throughout its range. Schooling behavior in young sharks makes entire juvenile cohorts vulnerable to a single net deployment.

threats.name: Misidentification in Trade
threats.description: Smooth hammerhead fins and meat are regularly misidentified as scalloped hammerhead or simply 'hammerhead' in landings and trade records, making it nearly impossible to assess true harvest levels for this species specifically.

threats.name: Slow Reproduction
threats.description: Female maturity at ~12 years and biennial breeding mean populations cannot quickly compensate for fishing mortality. Juvenile schooling aggregations are particularly attractive to fishers, removing large numbers before individuals can reproduce.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: As one of the more cold-tolerant hammerheads, warming seas may alter the distribution of inshore nursery grounds. Shifts in temperate prey availability could affect juvenile survival in key nursery areas.

actionItems.title: Oppose Hammerhead Fin Trade
actionItems.description: Smooth hammerhead fins are among the most commonly traded globally. Refuse shark fin products, advocate for fin trade bans, and support market campaigns to reduce demand in key consuming nations.

actionItems.title: Support CITES Appendix II Enforcement
actionItems.description: All three large hammerhead species are listed on CITES Appendix II. Push for stricter species-level identification in landings and exports — the current lumping of 'hammerhead' masks the true scale of smooth hammerhead harvest.

actionItems.title: Protect Juvenile Aggregation Sites
actionItems.description: Smooth hammerhead juveniles school in predictable inshore areas, making them highly vulnerable. Advocate for seasonal or permanent closures of known schooling and nursery sites to prevent targeted juvenile harvest.

actionItems.title: Fund Hammerhead Research
actionItems.description: Support organizations like the Save Our Seas Foundation and Shark Trust that fund tagging and population studies — essential given how poorly the smooth hammerhead's global status is understood relative to its congeners.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — depleted across all heavily fished regions

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — inshore nursery habitat under development and pollution pressure

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — coastal and pelagic prey fish heavily fished across its range

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — targeted for fins; juvenile schools especially vulnerable

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES II alongside other hammerheads; few species-specific measures

populationTrendMeta.note: Rough global estimates modeled from IUCN-documented declines of ~50% over three generations across the Atlantic and Indo-Pacific. The smooth hammerhead is more abundant than the great or scalloped hammerhead but no stock assessment exists. All figures carry high uncertainty.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal fish and stingray prey severely depleted; smooth hammerheads now rare

preyDeclineRegions.name: NE Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Schooling fish and benthic prey under intense commercial fishing pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish and rays declining across tropical and subtropical coastal zones

preyDeclineRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Caribbean and Gulf coastal prey fish significantly reduced

preyDeclineRegions.name: Australasia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Moderate pressure on coastal fish; some stocks managed

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted in artisanal and industrial fisheries; fins traded; no protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Fins highly valued; schooling juveniles regularly netted in large numbers

fishingPressureRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted and bycatch; species frequently lumped with other hammerheads in records

fishingPressureRegions.name: NE Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in longlines and trawls; CITES II applies but enforcement inconsistent

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australasia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Some bycatch in coastal nets; targeted in some artisanal fisheries
